overview: This week I rebuilt my Feature Spec Generator as a six-agent Streamlit system called the SAFe Feature Spec System, replacing the three-tab, manual clipboard workflow I'd been using. The system runs a full spec pipeline — classify, clarify, generate, review, improve, polish — in 10-15 minutes per spec at $0.15-0.25 in API costs, consistently hitting 90+ out of 100 on a rubric scorecard. Across all three test cases, I saw score deltas of +14 to +23 points, hitting the 4-5 docs per hour throughput leadership was asking for.

keyPoints.title: Manual workflow capped me at 2-3 feature docs per week
keyPoints.description: I was acting as the integration layer across three browser tabs, copying context by hand, which limited output even though my docs hit the 90% scorecard threshold.

keyPoints.title: Six-agent system produces 4-5 specs per hour at ~$0.20 per run
keyPoints.description: The pipeline uses Haiku for classification and Sonnet for the remaining five agents, completing each spec in 10-15 minutes for roughly $0.15-0.25 in API costs.

keyPoints.title: Streamlit session state replaced me as the pipeline's integration layer
keyPoints.description: Every piece of context that previously lived in my head or clipboard — feature type, interview answers, rubric decisions — became an explicit, machine-readable session variable.
keyPoints.sectionName: How to Replace Myself With Session State

keyPoints.title: Two-tier improvement threshold broke the 85-88 score ceiling
keyPoints.description: Tier 1 targets sections below 75% and Tier 2 auto-triggers a polish pass when the overall score lands between 80-89, pushing all three test cases to 92-94.

keyPoints.title: Boost inputs are the mechanism that makes AI output PM-quality
keyPoints.description: After the Reviewer scores the spec, I can paste additional domain context for sections below 75%, bridging the gap between competent AI output and a grounded specification.

keyPoints.title: Parse-once dictionary architecture prevented positional corruption on edits
keyPoints.description: Replacing positional string surgery with a parse-then-reassemble pattern meant editing one section by dictionary key could never cascade offsets into adjacent sections.

keyPoints.title: Improver regression on User Stories was the hardest debug of the program
keyPoints.description: The LLM's tendency to regenerate rather than edit caused scores to drop on untouched sections, and each attempted fix required a full pipeline run to validate before failing in a new way.
